reasoning: Sarah Sjöström currently holds the 50m freestyle long course world record with a blistering time of 23.61 from July 2023. She also clocked 23.66 at the 2024 Olympics. At the most recent major long course competition (World Aquatics Championships in Singapore, July/August 2025), the winning time was 24.02 by Meg Harris in Sjöström's absence. Top competitors like Gretchen Walsh are currently around the high 23.9s/low 24s in long course, which leaves a massive gap of ~0.3-0.4 seconds to Sjöström's record—an exceptionally large margin in a 50-meter event. Furthermore, the forecast horizon (November 2025 through February 2026) corresponds roughly to the winter swimming season, which is traditionally dominated by short course yards (US) or short course meters (global) competitions rather than long course events (though the U.S. Open and some Pro Swim Series events do take place). The absence of a major long course peak meet before February 2026 makes a record-breaking swim highly unlikely. The main minuscule risks include a surprise drop by an American at a winter long course meet or a completely unexpected retrospective disqualification of Sjöström's 2023 swim. As such, the probabilities are capped at the maximum allowed confidence (0.95) for the near term, gradually tapering to 0.92 for February 2026 to appropriately account for the slight increase in uncertainty over time.
